seo.title: Miss Jamaica's March 31 Payroll Deadline? Here's What Happens
seo.description: Missing Jamaica's March 31 fiscal year-end deadline triggers TAJ penalties, NHT surcharges, and audit risk. Learn the exact consequences and how to catch up fast.

title: What Happens If You Miss Jamaica's March 31 Payroll Deadline?
subtitle: TAJ penalties, NHT surcharges, and audit exposure — and what to do if you're already behind

ctaInline.headline: Check your payroll numbers before March 31
ctaInline.text: Use the free Jamaica payroll calculator

html:
March 31 is not just the end of a month in Jamaica — it is the close of the fiscal year, the statutory deadline for annual returns, and the date TAJ uses to measure whether your business is compliant. For Jamaican employers, missing this deadline is not a minor administrative slip. It triggers penalties, audit risk, and in some cases, formal TAJ assessments that can take months to resolve.

Here is exactly what happens when you miss the March 31 deadline, how serious each consequence is, and what you can do to minimise damage if you are already behind.

What Is Actually Due on March 31?

Three statutory obligations close on March 31 for Jamaican employers:

  • SO1 Annual Return (TAJ) — A summary of every employee's annual earnings, PAYE withheld, and statutory deductions. Required for every employee who worked for you during the tax year.
  • NHT Annual Return — The National Housing Trust's annual reconciliation of contributions. Must match your monthly NHT remittances throughout the year.
  • NIS Annual Certificate — National Insurance Scheme annual reconciliation confirming total contributions by employee.

Additionally, you should have your PAYE Year-End Reconciliation complete — this is not always a standalone filing but is reviewed as part of a TAJ audit.

Calculate your PAYE before it's too late
Use the free Jamaica payroll calculator to verify your deductions are correct before filing. No sign-up required.

The Penalty Structure: What TAJ and NHT Will Charge You

SO1 Late Filing Penalties

TAJ has clear penalty provisions for late or incorrect SO1 filings under the Income Tax Act:

ViolationPenalty
Late SO1 filing (per employee)Up to J$10,000 per employee
Incorrect PAYE calculationTax unpaid + 10% surcharge + interest
Failure to maintain recordsUp to J$100,000

For a business with 10 employees, a late SO1 filing can cost up to J$100,000 in penalties alone — before any back-tax liability is calculated.

NHT Late Contribution Penalties

The NHT penalty structure is arguably more aggressive:

  • Late NHT contributions: 20% flat surcharge on the outstanding amount
  • Unpaid employer contributions (3% of gross): Full 20% penalty applies
  • Example: If you owe J$50,000 in NHT contributions, late payment adds J$10,000 automatically

NHT interest compounds monthly on unpaid balances. A one-month delay can become significantly more expensive if not resolved quickly.

NIS Late Filing

NIS penalties are applied on unpaid contributions:

  • Late contributions attract a surcharge plus interest at the prescribed rate
  • NIS can also bar non-compliant employers from certain government contracts

What a TAJ Audit Actually Looks Like

Missing the March 31 deadline or filing incorrect returns increases your audit risk. TAJ payroll audits are not random — they are triggered by mismatches between:

  • Monthly SO1 returns vs annual SO1 summary
  • NHT records vs SO1 records
  • NIS records vs SO1 records
  • Bank remittance receipts vs declared payroll

When TAJ conducts a payroll audit, they review up to 3 years of records. A single year's error can open the door to scrutiny of prior years. The audit process typically involves:

  1. TAJ issues a formal notice requesting records within 14 days
  2. You must produce payroll records, bank statements, payslips, and all S01 receipts
  3. TAJ calculates the tax liability based on what they find
  4. An assessment is issued — you pay or dispute via formal objection
  5. If you dispute, the process can take 6-18 months to resolve

The cost of an audit is not just the penalties — it is the legal fees, accountant time, and management distraction of responding to TAJ for months.

What If You're Already Behind?

If March 31 is approaching and you have gaps in your payroll records or missing remittances, act immediately. Here is the priority order:

Step 1: Identify the gaps

Pull every SO1 receipt, NHT receipt, and NIS payment confirmation for the year. Compare to each pay period. Document exactly what was filed and what was not.

Step 2: File what you can

TAJ and NHT typically treat voluntary late filing more favourably than non-filing discovered in an audit. File late returns now. The penalty for voluntary late filing is usually lower than for audit-discovered non-compliance.

Step 3: Calculate what you owe

Use a Jamaica-compliant payroll calculator to recalculate every employee's deductions for the year using the correct 2025/2026 rates:

  • PAYE threshold: J$1,500,096/year (J$125,008/month)
  • NIS: 3% employee + 3% employer
  • NHT: 2% employee + 3% employer
  • Education Tax: 2.25% employee + 3.5% employer
  • PAYE rate: 25% on income above threshold up to J$6M, 30% above J$6M

Step 4: Make payments with interest

Pay any outstanding contributions as quickly as possible. The surcharges increase the longer you wait.

Step 5: Get proper software for next year

If you are scrambling in March, the root cause is usually manual payroll or non-compliant software. Jamaica-specific payroll software automatically generates SO1 data, tracks contributions throughout the year, and makes March 31 a 30-minute filing — not a crisis.

Common errors that trigger TAJ assessments:
  • Using the wrong PAYE threshold (J$1,825,000 is incorrect — the 2025/26 threshold is J$1,500,096)
  • Calculating PAYE before deducting NIS from gross pay
  • Missing NHT on bonus or commission payments
  • Not deducting Education Tax from part-time or contract workers who cross the threshold

The Bottom Line

Missing Jamaica's March 31 payroll deadline is expensive, time-consuming, and stressful. The penalty structure favours businesses that file on time — even imperfectly — over those that do not file at all. If you are behind, start today. If you are on track, verify your numbers before you file.

Use the free PayrollJamaica calculator to confirm your deductions are correct before submitting your annual returns. It handles all four statutory deductions in the correct order — no sign-up required.
